articles.title: The Cocoa Crash Is a Buying Window, Not a Bottom
articles.description: A Signal Fire on cocoa: futures fell about 60% from their 2024 peak, but disease (swollen shoot in 41% of surveyed Ivory Coast farms) and a warming West African belt have raised the floor for good. The read for the galley — buy dark couverture on the soft bean price, not the shelf price that never fell, and hold it right.
articles.lede: A Signal Fire on cocoa: futures fell about 60% from their 2024 peak, but disease (swollen shoot in 41% of surveyed Ivory Coast farms) and a warming West African belt have raised the floor for good. The read for the galley — buy dark couverture on the soft bean price, not the shelf price that never fell, and hold it right.

articles.title: The Green Purée: Holding the Colour
articles.lede: A bright, spoon-coating green purée that stays green from pass to plate. The whole trick is a hard blanch, a hard chill, and never letting acid near it until the last second.

articles.title: Prawn Shells, Fermented Into Umami
articles.lede: A 2026 Singapore study fed prawn by-products to six microbes. One mould turned shell-and-head waste into a glutamate load worth six grams of MSG per hundred.

articles.title: Cultivated Foie Gras Nears the Galley
articles.lede: A duck liver grown from cells — no bird force-fed — cleared its first market in 2026. For a charter galley that plates foie gras by the kilo, the question is shifting from whether to which guest asks first.

articles.title: The Preference Sheet: Reading the Document That Runs the Job
articles.lede: A dislike is a preference; an intolerance is a safety brief. The sheet rarely tells you which — so you learn to read it like a chart, not a wish list.

articles.title: The Tuna Bloodline
articles.lede: The dark stripe most cooks trim and bin is the most concentrated flavour on the fish. Treat it right and it becomes seasoning, not waste.

articles.title: The Sorrento Lemon
articles.lede: A single oval lemon from terraced cliffs above the Tyrrhenian, its peel more than seventy per cent limonene — and why a yacht chef should buy it by name.

articles.title: Two Jobs, One Salary: The Chef-Stew Hybrid, Decoded
articles.lede: ’80 percent cook, 20 percent stew’ is not a splash of extra duties. It is two positions paid as one, and the rest-hours ceiling is where it breaks.

articles.title: Thawing Fish Without the Drip: The Cold-Brine Method
articles.lede: Air-thawing costs you colour and up to a third of the fish’s water. A cold salt bath thaws faster, holds the flesh red, and keeps the surface out of the danger zone.

articles.title: The Cheap Fish Go First: Sardine and Anchovy Before Bluefin
articles.description: A Horizon forecast: in the north-western Mediterranean the cheap oily fish that feed the crew have the shakier decade ahead, while eastern Atlantic bluefin sits at its highest spawning biomass since the 1960s. Three named signals, a falsifiable call, and the provisioning moves to make now.
articles.lede: A Horizon forecast: in the north-western Mediterranean the cheap oily fish that feed the crew have the shakier decade ahead, while eastern Atlantic bluefin sits at its highest spawning biomass since the 1960s. Three named signals, a falsifiable call, and the provisioning moves to make now.

articles.title: The Guacamole That Does Not Brown
articles.lede: The lime is not just for taste. Drop the pH under four and the enzyme that turns guacamole grey stops working.

articles.title: Miso From Scratch: Koji, Salt, and Time
articles.lede: Buy a paste and you buy someone else’s decisions. Build it from koji and you control the salt, the sweetness, and how much glutamate the mould sets free.

articles.title: The Menu Agent: Turn Any AI Into a Superyacht Menu Designer
articles.description: A ~985-line prompt turns Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini into a superyacht menu designer: every menu anchored to a named touchstone, a self-improving palette memory so no two cards repeat, a non-overridable validation gate, and a one-page card you print from any browser.
articles.lede: A ~985-line prompt turns Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini into a superyacht menu designer: every menu anchored to a named touchstone, a self-improving palette memory so no two cards repeat, a non-overridable validation gate, and a one-page card you print from any browser.

articles.title: Two Eggs, Two Temperatures
articles.description: A firm poached white needs ~80°C; a custard yolk locks at ~63°C — so no single bath does both. The protein chemistry and the make-ahead poach-shock-hold-reheat line that plates a hundred eggs.
articles.lede: A firm poached white needs ~80°C; a custard yolk locks at ~63°C — so no single bath does both. The protein chemistry and the make-ahead poach-shock-hold-reheat line that plates a hundred eggs.

articles.title: One Dial, Every Grain
articles.description: One setpoint for every grain: 100% steam, 100°C. Vary only the water ratio and the minutes. A grain-by-grain reference table, the science of one dial, and the par-cook-and-regenerate line for service.
articles.lede: One setpoint for every grain: 100% steam, 100°C. Vary only the water ratio and the minutes. A grain-by-grain reference table, the science of one dial, and the par-cook-and-regenerate line for service.

articles.title: Taking a Hard Shot Without Flinching
articles.description: The burn of a neat spirit is not flavour but a heat alarm ethanol resets to your own mouth temperature — and the cold, breath and one-swallow technique that switches it off, honest about where the evidence stops.
articles.lede: The burn of a neat spirit is not flavour but a heat alarm ethanol resets to your own mouth temperature — and the cold, breath and one-swallow technique that switches it off, honest about where the evidence stops.

articles.title: MSG, Honestly: Using Glutamate to Cut the Salt Without Losing the Flavour
articles.description: Few ingredients carry a worse reputation on weaker evidence than monosodium glutamate. It is the most-studied way to cut a dish's sodium without flattening it — and the science that condemned it never held up. Here is what glutamate actually is, the salt it saves on a long charter, and the one honest caveat worth keeping.
articles.lede: Few ingredients carry a worse reputation on weaker evidence than monosodium glutamate. It is the most-studied way to cut a dish's sodium without flattening it — and the science that condemned it never held up. Here is what glutamate actually is, the salt it saves on a long charter, and the one honest caveat worth keeping.

articles.title: The Pairing Does the Healing
articles.description: A single ingredient rarely heals. The body absorbs most active compounds only as members of a team — and the cook decides whether the team turns up. This is the working chef's guide to the combinations that actually reach the blood, the ones that quietly cancel each other, the heat that…
articles.lede: A single ingredient rarely heals. The body absorbs most active compounds only as members of a team — and the cook decides whether the team turns up. This is the working chef's guide to the combinations that actually reach the blood, the ones that quietly cancel each other, the heat that…

articles.title: The Smell of Cooking: Why Onions Seduce, Broccoli Turns, and Aromas Linger
articles.description: A pan of onions browning in fat is the most reliable seduction in any galley — and a sprout boiled five minutes too long is the fastest way to lose a saloon. Both are the same kind of event: trace molecules, detected at concentrations your kitchen scale could never weigh, read by a nose built to care. Here is the chemistry of cooking aroma — why the good smells pull, why the bad ones repel, and why neither leaves your curtains when you ask.
articles.lede: A pan of onions browning in fat is the most reliable seduction in any galley — and a sprout boiled five minutes too long is the fastest way to lose a saloon. Both are the same kind of event: trace molecules, detected at concentrations your kitchen scale could never weigh, read by a nose built to care. Here is the chemistry of cooking aroma — why the good smells pull, why the bad ones repel, and why neither leaves your curtains when you ask.

articles.title: How Many Senses You Have, and How Many Sit Down to Eat
articles.description: The "five senses" is a 2,300-year-old shorthand, not the instrument panel your guest is eating with. Here is the honest count, and the eight or more senses that actually build a plate of flavour.
articles.lede: The "five senses" is a 2,300-year-old shorthand, not the instrument panel your guest is eating with. Here is the honest count, and the eight or more senses that actually build a plate of flavour.

articles.title: The Preservative Paradox
articles.lede: A 112,000-person study just linked eight common food preservatives — including added citric acid and ascorbic acid — to higher blood pressure and heart risk. What it means for a galley that runs on long-life provisioning, and what it doesn’t.

articles.title: The Burger Grind
articles.lede: Pre-ground mince is one muscle, one fat level, ground to mush. The galley fix is three cuts, two grinds and a thermometer — a patty with the bite, fat and beef depth a single-cut grind can’t reach.

articles.title: The Parting Gift: What Guests Take Home From the Galley
articles.lede: What to send a guest home with - the small, edible, personal thing that outlasts the charter.

articles.title: The Parmigiano Wheel: Tableside Theatre, Judged
articles.lede: Parmigiano wheels, bamboo steamers, dushi boats, slate, salt blocks - the serving vessel is the first thing the guest tastes with their eyes.

articles.title: Whiplash: A Fermented Hot Sauce With a Sting
articles.lede: A lacto-fermented hot sauce engineered for a clean, building heat - the one the crew steals from the galley.

articles.title: Flavours That Stay in the Mouth
articles.lede: Aged Comte, chorizo, Epoisses, vin jaune, absinthe — why some flavours sit in the mouth long after the bite, and how to build that persistence on purpose.

articles.title: Eggs: What Every Dish Asks of Them
articles.lede: When to stop whisking, when to salt, what heat does to the white before the yolk — the egg, decoded dish by dish.

articles.title: What Yacht Chefs Actually Earn: A 2026 Pay Map
articles.lede: The honest 2026 yacht-chef salary map: base pay by vessel size, every package perk priced, the guests-aboard workload, the job ranked by demand, and the modifiers (solo vs teamed, private vs charter, experience) that set your number. Plus a build-your-number worksheet.

articles.title: The Equipment Verdict: What Survives a Season at Sea
articles.lede: The galley runs on a handful of machines, and the fleet has hard opinions earned at sea. The gear that survives a season, the gear that dies on arrival day, and the one rule that keeps you cooking when a unit fails mid-charter.

articles.title: Cooking for the Ozempic Guest
articles.lede: More guests now board on a GLP-1 drug, and a rich tasting menu is exactly the wrong plan. The science of why appetite collapses on Ozempic — and the small, low-fat, protein-first service that keeps them comfortable, and fed.

articles.title: Cooking the Atlantic Crossing: The Galley Playbook
articles.lede: Three weeks of provisioning, twenty crew, two thousand miles and a galley that pitches. An ocean crossing is won before you leave the dock — the freezer strategy, the rough-weather meals, and the morale that quietly runs on baking and snacks.

articles.title: Cook the Port: Terroir as a Galley Workflow
articles.lede: Most ports hand you one ingredient worth building the menu around. Cooking the place you are in — terroir as a workflow, not a romance — makes provisioning easier, the plate better, and the markup in Monaco optional.

articles.title: Sourdough at Sea: The Science-First Loaf
articles.lede: An open, blistered crumb and a deep mahogany crust — raised on a jar of flour and water you keep alive yourself. The wild loaf made reliable on a moving galley, where the acidity that flavours it also makes it keep. Interactive: scale it, tick it, cook it.

articles.title: Sea Urchin: The Candy of the Sea
articles.lede: Uni is a reproductive organ that tastes of sugar — and the reason why is two amino acids, glycine and alanine. The full profile: the candy chemistry, the alum trick that hides age, the four ways to use it, and how to keep something this perishable alive on charter.

articles.title: elBulli: The Coastline That Rewrote Cooking
articles.lede: A beach bar named after two French bulldogs became the most influential kitchen of the last century — then closed at its peak to protect the one thing it was built for. The frontier elBulli explored, the chemistry it left behind, and what a galley can still steal from it.

articles.title: The One-Hour Lunch: Market Meals That Feel Special on Provisioning Day
articles.lede: Provisioning day, one hour to plate lunch, a galley full of boxes. The move isn’t to cook faster — it’s to let the market do the prep and assemble with intention. Here’s the playbook.

articles.title: Koji: The Mould That Cooks For You
articles.description: One microbe turns rice into sweetness, protein into umami, and a tough cut into something tender — before a flame is ever lit. Meet the fungus Japan made its national organism.
articles.lede: One microbe turns rice into sweetness, protein into umami, and a tough cut into something tender — before a flame is ever lit. Meet the fungus Japan made its national organism.

articles.title: Oven Hash Browns: The Sheet-Pan Slab
articles.lede: One sheet-pan slab, shatter-crisp on both faces, tender inside — for a crew of ten to twelve. The trick is water: rinse, salt, squeeze bone-dry, then a hot preheated pan does the rest. Interactive: scale it, tick it, cook it.

articles.title: Focaccia: The Science-First Version
articles.lede: 80% hydration, a 24–72h cold ferment, and a salamoia brine in the dimples. The open, custardy crumb and crisp-tender Ligurian crust — made reliable. Interactive: scale it, tick it, cook it.

articles.title: The Crew Quiche: The Science-First Version
articles.lede: The appareil ratio that sets silky yet sliceable, a blind-baked sealed shell, and a low gentle bake pulled at a jiggle. A crew quiche for ten in a half gastronorm. Interactive: scale it, tick it, cook it.

articles.title: Pita Bread: The Science-First Version
articles.lede: 64% hydration, a screaming-hot surface, and a relaxed rest after rolling. The steam-leavened pocket made reliable — on a stone or a galley skillet. Twenty pitas for ten to twelve. Interactive: scale it, tick it, cook it.

articles.title: Moist Carrot Cake: The Science-First Version
articles.lede: Toasted-walnut butter, macerated carrot, mascarpone-tempered cream cheese frosting. Three science-backed modifications that produce the moistest, most stable carrot cake you will ever serve on charter. Every step tested, every claim sourced.

articles.title: Sticky Toffee Pudding: Dark Muscovado & Whisky Caramel
articles.lede: Dark muscovado, soda-bloomed dates, whisky caramel. Three modifications grounded in food chemistry that turn nursery dessert into a charter-grade course.

articles.title: Garlic: The Immortal Bulb
articles.lede: 7,000 years of cultivation. Found in Tutankhamun's tomb. Fed to Olympic athletes. Feared by vampires. Five forms in your galley. The complete guide to the most important ingredient in human history.

articles.title: Mushrooms: The New Centre of the Plate
articles.lede: Shiitakes replace steak. King oyster sears like escalope. A good mushroom has the umami of aged meat and the texture of a noble product. The complete guide to the ingredient rewriting the centre of the plate.

articles.title: Tea as a Cooking Ingredient
articles.lede: Not tea as a beverage. Tea as a cooking ingredient. Hojicha in sauces, lapsang souchong in jus, matcha in marinades. The aromatic complexity that classical fonds cannot reproduce.

articles.title: Apple Crumble: The Perfect Version
articles.lede: Two apple varieties. A 71°C pre-cook that activates pectin methylesterase. Brown-butter oat crumble. Flaky sea salt on top. Every failure mode fixed structurally.

articles.title: Chicken Soup: The Perfect Version
articles.lede: Cold-start extraction from roasted bones. Chicken feet for gelatin. Meat poached separately, never boiled into cotton. Fish sauce and parmesan rind for invisible umami. Every step sourced.

articles.title: Banana Bread: The Science-First Version
articles.lede: Brown butter, roasted bananas, white miso. Three science-backed modifications that transform banana bread. Every step tested, every claim sourced.

articles.title: Port Call: Provisioning Guides Built by the Chefs Who Actually Dock There
articles.lede: Port Call is the provisioning playbook series: three berths, real suppliers with phone + address, inline cautions, watch-outs by season. Venice + Miami live. St. Barth, Antibes, Palma incoming.

articles.title: Signal Fire: One Chef, One Idea That Changes Everything
articles.lede: Signal Fire: one chef, one idea that changes everything. Not a profile. A dissection of a single principle that survived decades in kitchens. Coming soon.

articles.title: The Locker: Deep Pantry Guides for Chefs Who Stock Like an Arsenal
articles.lede: The Locker: deep pantry guides for chefs who stock like an arsenal. Specific brands, specific reasons, three tiers. Coming soon.

articles.title: Port Call Venice: Provisioning a City With No Roads
articles.lede: Port Call Venice: the three berths, Pesto Sea Group + the agent call, the Rialto Pescheria at dawn, Sant'Erasmo direct-supply, Mestre as the wholesale answer. Watch-outs for Biennale, Redentore, acqua alta, Ferragosto. Built from working yacht-chef intel.

articles.title: FlavorDB: The Cheat Code for Flavor Pairing
articles.lede: FlavorDB maps 25,595 flavor molecules across 936 ingredients. Here's how to use it to build dishes based on shared volatile compounds — not tradition, not intuition, but chemistry.

articles.title: Claude Code in the Galley: The Power Tool
articles.lede: Claude Code turns your galley into a system — PDF menu cards, guest intelligence from WhatsApp exports, automated handover notes, and a shared brain between rotational chefs.

articles.title: The Truth About Paying Tax as Yacht Crew
articles.lede: The truth about paying tax as yacht crew. What Europeans, Americans, South Africans, and Australians actually owe — and what most people get dangerously wrong.

articles.title: How to Become a Yacht Chef: The Real Path Nobody Advertises
articles.lede: The real path to becoming a yacht chef. Certifications, day work, crew agencies, what to cook at a trial, and what nobody tells you before your first season.

articles.title: Technology in the Galley: Your AI Sous Chef
articles.lede: AI is the most powerful galley tool since the immersion circulator. Here's how to use it for menus, provisioning, guest preferences, and recipe development — with a ready-to-use menu agent you can copy into any AI system.

articles.title: WhatsApp as Your Trip Intelligence System
articles.lede: Two WhatsApp groups. One chat export. Every guest preference, every broken gasket, every handover note — captured automatically, timestamped, and ready to process with AI. The complete trip-closing system for yacht chefs.

articles.title: Provisioning Miami & Fort Lauderdale: The Complete Playbook
articles.lede: The complete provisioning playbook for Miami and Fort Lauderdale. Three berths, wholesale infrastructure, off-the-boat fish, the Hialeah Asian stock-up, season-aware cautions. Built from working yacht-chef intel.

articles.title: The Fat Index: Every Cooking Fat Ranked by Science
articles.lede: 17 cooking fats ranked by nutrient density, cooking versatility, omega profile, and evidence base. The definitive science-backed reference for your galley.

articles.title: The Salt Index: Every Culinary Salt Ranked by Science
articles.lede: Every culinary salt compared by mineral content, crystal structure, dissolution rate, and practical application. The definitive science-backed salt reference.

articles.title: The Acid Index: Every Culinary Acid Ranked by Science
articles.lede: Every culinary acid ranked by pH, flavour profile, heat stability, and practical application. Citrus, vinegars, fermented acids, and mineral acids — the definitive reference.

articles.title: Cooked: How Fire Made Us Human and Why the Person Holding the Pan Still Matters
articles.lede: Without cooked food, no human brains, no culture, no civilisation. The science of how fire made us who we are — and why the person holding the pan still matters.

articles.title: Galley Efficiency Protocol: Maximizing Output in Minimal Space
articles.lede: The complete system for organizing workflow, station setup, and mise en place in confined yacht galleys. Tested across 50+ vessels.

articles.title: Sea Urchin: Seasonal Guide, Compound Profile, and Sourcing
articles.lede: Sea urchin compound profile, seasonal peak data, and sourcing guidelines for yacht chefs. Paracentrotus lividus at pre-spawning maximum.

articles.title: Sea Urchin Beurre Blanc: Roe as Natural Emulsifier
articles.lede: Use raw sea urchin roe to stabilise a beurre blanc without cream or starch. The roe's phospholipids do the work while its glutamic acid amplifies umami.

articles.title: The Silver Lining Protocol: A Science-Backed Survival Guide for Galley Sanity
articles.lede: 82% of superyacht crew report low mood or anxiety onboard. The psychology of submarines, Antarctic stations, and NASA missions offers a tested playbook.

articles.title: The Pharmacy in Your Spice Rack: What Science Actually Knows About Medicinal Plants in Food
articles.lede: Cacao, turmeric, saffron, cinnamon — the plants that shaped civilisations and still protect us. The science behind why ancient ingredients work.

articles.title: Umami in Western Cuisine: Fermentation as the Universal Glutamate Engine
articles.lede: Fermentation is the universal mechanism for generating free glutamate. Western food traditions have been doing it for centuries. The data, the galley implications, and the limitations.

articles.title: Spaghetti ai Ricci di Mare: Mondello, Sicily
articles.lede: Spaghetti ai ricci di mare from Mondello, Sicily. Four ingredients, one coastline, no shortcuts. The dish exists because sea urchins were free.

articles.title: Myojaku Earns Three Stars and STCW Anti-Harassment Training Goes Mandatory
articles.lede: Myojaku earns three Michelin stars by stripping umami back to the ingredient itself. Plus: STCW anti-harassment training goes mandatory for all flag states.
